text: This video vignette shows tip and tricks to increase the surgical success rate of the Marcius approach for the repair of Crohn's-related recurrent low rectovaginal fistula.
The procedure was performed under spinal anesthesia with the patient in the lithotomy position.

A vertical incision is made at the level of the vaginal introitus, allowing for careful dissection of the rectovaginal septum. The septum is circumferentially freed from the orifice of the fistula to facilitate repair.
The surgeon repairs the fistula from the vaginal side using interrupted 2-0 absorbable sutures, following curettage of the fistula orifice from the rectal side.

A vertical left paravaginal incision, seven centimeters in length, is made to access the bulbocavernosus muscle. The muscle is carefully mobilized along with its surrounding fibroadipose tissue, while preserving its posterolateral blood supply from the internal pudendal artery.
An important trick for success is to be sure that an adequate length of the flap has been achieved before its superior intersection.
The surgeon transposes the isolated bulbocavernosus muscle into the previously prepared subcutaneous tunnel, ensuring the muscle's well-vascularized tissue provides protection for the direct repair.
The surgeon cuts the distal limb of the flap to prevent excessive bulking of the muscle at the rectovaginal septum, which could potentially cause dyspareunia.
The surgeon fixes the graft in place using interrupted 2-0 absorbable sutures and closes the vertical paravaginal incision. A pain rose drain is left in place to manage postoperative discomfort.

The postoperative period was uneventful and after a new evaluation under anesthesia confirming the healing of the rectovaginal fistula, the ostomy was closed three months after surgery. No recurrence nor any episode of dyspareunia occurred.
